title: kubectl Cheat Sheet
description: 80+ kubectl commands with flags, real-world use cases, and gotchas. Searchable and filterable. Verified against official Kubernetes docs.

incidentFlow.title: Production Incident Quick-Flow
incidentFlow.subtitle: The exact sequence engineers reach for first when something breaks in production. Run these in order before doing anything else.

steps.why: Get a cluster-wide view first before assuming the pod is in the namespace you expect. The most common beginner confusion is a pod that deployed to the wrong namespace. -A removes that guesswork immediately.

Read the Events section at the bottom. This is where Kubernetes tells you the actual reason something failed. kubectl get pods only shows a status label. describe shows whether it is OOMKilled, ImagePullBackOff, a failed probe, or a scheduling failure.
If the pod already restarted, plain kubectl logs shows the new container's empty logs. --previous shows what the crashed instance printed right before it died. This is the flag people forget at 2am.

Cluster events expire after about an hour so check this early. Sorting by timestamp puts them in chronological order. Without sorting the default output order is almost useless for incident diagnosis.

If a recent deploy caused the incident this is the fastest path back to a known-good state. Faster than finding and re-applying the last-good manifest. Only works if the ReplicaSet history has not been garbage collected.

podStatusDecoder.title: Pod Status Decoder
podStatusDecoder.subtitle: What the STATUS column in kubectl get pods is actually telling you and where to look next.

rows.meaning: Pod accepted by the cluster but not yet scheduled to a node.
rows.action: kubectl describe pod <name> and read the Events section. Look for insufficient CPU or memory, unsatisfied node selectors, or taints without matching tolerations.

rows.meaning: The kubelet cannot pull the container image from the registry.
rows.action: Check the image name and tag for typos. Confirm the registry is reachable. Verify an imagePullSecret is attached to the pod spec if it is a private registry.

rows.meaning: The container starts, crashes immediately, and Kubernetes is backing off between restart attempts.
rows.action: kubectl logs <name> --previous to see what the crashed instance printed. Then kubectl describe pod <name> for the exit code and Events section. OOMKilled exit code 137 means memory limit hit.

rows.meaning: The container exceeded its memory limit and the Linux kernel killed it.
rows.action: kubectl top pod <name> --containers to see current memory usage. Either raise the memory limit in the pod spec or investigate a memory leak in the application.

rows.meaning: The pod spec references a ConfigMap or Secret key that does not exist in this namespace.
rows.action: kubectl get configmap and kubectl get secret to confirm the referenced objects and keys actually exist in the correct namespace.

rows.meaning: The container process is running but failing its readiness probe so the pod is not receiving traffic.
rows.action: kubectl describe pod <name> to see the readiness probe failure reason and threshold counts. The app is alive but cannot serve traffic yet.

commands.title: View cluster info
commands.useCase: First command to run against a new cluster context to confirm kubectl can actually reach the API server before doing anything else.
commands.beginnerNote: This only shows the control plane URL, not overall cluster health. A clean cluster-info output does not mean every node or workload in the cluster is healthy.

commands.title: Check kubectl and cluster version

flags.description: Show only the local kubectl client version without contacting the cluster

commands.useCase: Confirm client and server version skew before applying manifests that depend on a specific API version or feature gate.
commands.beginnerNote: Kubernetes only officially supports a client within one minor version of the server. A big skew can cause apply errors that look unrelated to versioning.

commands.title: List cluster nodes

flags.description: Add internal/external IP, OS image, kernel version and container runtime columns

commands.useCase: Quick health check of every node in the cluster before digging into a scheduling or capacity issue.
commands.beginnerNote: Node STATUS of Ready only reflects a recent kubelet heartbeat, not whether the node actually has spare CPU or memory capacity for more pods.

commands.title: List namespaces
commands.useCase: See every namespace in the cluster when you are not sure where a workload was deployed.

commands.title: Create a namespace
commands.useCase: Set up an isolated namespace for a new team, environment, or application before deploying anything into it.
commands.beginnerNote: Namespaces do not provide network isolation by default. Pods in different namespaces can still talk to each other unless a NetworkPolicy explicitly blocks it.

commands.title: Delete a namespace
commands.useCase: Tear down an entire environment, such as a temporary review-app or staging namespace, in one command.
commands.beginnerNote: This deletes EVERY resource inside the namespace with no recovery option. This is the most dangerous command on this entire cheat sheet — double check kubectl config current-context first.

commands.title: List available contexts
commands.useCase: See every cluster/user/namespace combination saved in your kubeconfig before switching between clusters.
commands.beginnerNote: The asterisk in the first column marks your CURRENT context. Always check this before running any destructive command — it is the single fastest sanity check that exists.

commands.title: Switch active context
commands.useCase: Switch kubectl to point at a different cluster, such as moving from staging to production.
commands.beginnerNote: This is the number one cause of accidentally deleting or modifying something in the wrong cluster. Always confirm with kubectl config current-context after switching.

commands.title: Show current context
commands.useCase: A sanity check before running any risky command — confirm you are actually pointed at the cluster you think you are.

commands.title: Set default namespace for current context
commands.useCase: Avoid typing -n <namespace> on every single command when you are working in one namespace for an extended session.
commands.beginnerNote: This changes the default namespace stored in your kubeconfig, not just for the current terminal session — it persists until you change it again.

commands.title: View kubeconfig

flags.description: Include actual embedded credentials (certificates and tokens) instead of redacted placeholders

commands.useCase: Inspect which clusters, users and contexts are merged into your active kubeconfig, especially after merging multiple config files.
commands.beginnerNote: The --raw flag includes actual credentials in plaintext. Never paste --raw output into a public chat, ticket, or AI chat tool.

commands.title: Cordon a node
commands.useCase: Mark a node unschedulable before performing maintenance, so no new pods land on it while you investigate or patch it.
commands.beginnerNote: Cordon alone does NOT evict existing pods already running on the node — it only blocks new ones from being scheduled there. Pair it with drain to actually empty the node.

commands.title: Drain a node

flags.description: Required — otherwise drain refuses to proceed because DaemonSet-managed pods cannot be safely evicted
Required if any pod uses an emptyDir volume, since that data cannot survive eviction anyway

commands.useCase: Safely evict all pods from a node before decommissioning it or performing disruptive maintenance like a kernel upgrade.
commands.beginnerNote: Drain can hang indefinitely waiting for a PodDisruptionBudget to allow eviction. If it seems stuck, check kubectl get pdb -A for a budget blocking the last available replica.

commands.title: Uncordon a node
commands.useCase: Mark a node schedulable again after maintenance is complete so the scheduler starts placing pods on it once more.

commands.title: Taint a node
commands.useCase: Reserve a node for specific workloads, such as GPU jobs or a dedicated tenant, by repelling everything else.
commands.beginnerNote: A taint repels pods without a matching toleration but does NOT attract pods to that node. To actually steer specific workloads onto it, combine the taint with nodeAffinity in the pod spec.

commands.title: Check control plane component status (deprecated)
commands.useCase: Historically used to check whether the scheduler, controller-manager, and etcd were reporting healthy.
commands.beginnerNote: Deprecated since v1.19 and unreliable on managed clusters like EKS, GKE, and AKS, where it often just returns Unknown for every component. Use cloud-provider-specific health dashboards instead.

commands.title: List pods

flags.description: List pods across all namespaces instead of just the current one
Add node, pod IP, and QoS class columns

commands.useCase: The most basic health check — see what pods exist and their current status in a namespace.
commands.beginnerNote: Running this with no flags only shows the default namespace. Use -A if your pod seems to have disappeared — it is almost always sitting in a different namespace.

commands.title: List all common resources in a namespace
commands.useCase: Get a quick overview of pods, services, deployments, and replicasets in the current namespace at once.
commands.beginnerNote: Despite the name, get all does NOT return ConfigMaps, Secrets, Ingress objects, or custom resources. This trips up nearly every beginner who assumes 'all' really means all.

commands.title: Run a throwaway debug pod
commands.useCase: Spin up a throwaway debug pod to test DNS resolution or network connectivity from inside the cluster, then have it clean itself up automatically.
commands.beginnerNote: Since Kubernetes v1.18, kubectl run only creates a plain Pod, not a Deployment. Older tutorials that assume it creates a Deployment are out of date.

commands.title: Add a label to a pod

flags.description: Required if the label key already exists on the resource, otherwise the command errors out

commands.useCase: Tag pods so they can be selected by a Service, NetworkPolicy, or filtered with kubectl get pods -l.

commands.title: Add an annotation to a pod
commands.useCase: Attach non-identifying metadata, like a build hash or deploy timestamp, that a CI tool or dashboard reads later.
commands.beginnerNote: Labels are for selection and filtering, annotations are for arbitrary metadata that tooling reads. Mixing them up — like trying to select on an annotation — is a common conceptual error.

commands.title: Delete a pod
commands.useCase: Force a single unhealthy pod to restart when you suspect it is stuck in a bad state, such as a hung connection pool.
commands.beginnerNote: If the pod is managed by a Deployment or ReplicaSet it will be recreated within seconds. Deleting the pod does not delete the underlying workload that owns it.

commands.title: Copy a file into a pod
commands.useCase: Push a config file, certificate, or patched binary into a running container without rebuilding the image.
commands.beginnerNote: Requires tar to be installed inside the target container. It silently fails or hangs on minimal or distroless images that do not have tar available.

commands.title: Copy a file out of a pod
commands.useCase: Pull a log file, heap dump, or generated report off a container for local inspection.
commands.beginnerNote: Same tar dependency as copying into a pod — this also requires tar inside the source container or it will fail with an unclear error.

commands.title: List deployments
commands.useCase: Check replica counts and rollout status for every deployment in the current namespace at a glance.

commands.title: Apply a manifest
commands.useCase: The standard way to create or update any resource declaratively from a YAML manifest, ideal for GitOps and CI pipelines.
commands.beginnerNote: Apply computes a diff against the last-applied-configuration annotation. If someone hand-edited the resource with kubectl edit, this can produce confusing three-way merge conflicts.

commands.title: Create a resource from a manifest
commands.useCase: Create a brand-new resource once, in a one-off script where you specifically want it to fail if the resource already exists.
commands.beginnerNote: Unlike apply, create errors out with AlreadyExists if the resource already exists — it has no built-in update behaviour.

commands.title: Scale a deployment
commands.useCase: Quickly add or remove replicas to handle a traffic spike or scheduled maintenance window.
commands.beginnerNote: If a HorizontalPodAutoscaler is managing this deployment, it can override your manual scale within seconds as it reconciles back to its target.

commands.title: Create a HorizontalPodAutoscaler
commands.useCase: Automatically scale replica count between a floor and ceiling based on observed CPU utilization.
commands.beginnerNote: Requires metrics-server to be installed in the cluster. Without it, the HPA shows unknown targets in kubectl get hpa and never scales anything.

commands.title: Update a deployment's container image
commands.useCase: Deploy a new image tag from a CI pipeline without re-applying the whole manifest.

commands.title: Patch a resource
commands.useCase: Modify a single field on a live resource, such as replica count or an annotation, without touching the rest of the spec.
commands.beginnerNote: Quoting JSON correctly in a patch is a frequent source of shell-escaping errors, especially on Windows where quote characters behave differently between cmd, PowerShell, and Git Bash.

commands.title: Edit a resource live
commands.useCase: Make a quick emergency change directly against the live object when there is no time to update a manifest and re-apply.
commands.beginnerNote: Changes apply immediately on save with no confirmation step. Edits made this way are not tracked in version control and will be silently overwritten the next time kubectl apply runs.

commands.title: Replace a resource
commands.useCase: Force a full replacement of a resource's spec when apply's three-way merge is producing unwanted results.
commands.beginnerNote: Requires the full current spec or it fails outright. Most engineers should use apply instead — replace is a niche escape hatch, not a default.

commands.title: Delete a deployment
commands.useCase: Remove a deployment and all of its managed ReplicaSets and Pods, such as decommissioning an old service.
commands.beginnerNote: This is a hard delete with no confirmation prompt and no built-in undo. Make sure the manifest is still in version control before running it.

commands.title: Check rollout status
commands.useCase: Block a CI/CD pipeline until a rolling update has fully finished, so the next pipeline step only runs against healthy pods.
commands.beginnerNote: Hangs indefinitely if the rollout is stuck, unless you set --timeout. This can silently freeze a CI job for hours until it hits an external timeout.

commands.title: View rollout history
commands.useCase: See every revision of a deployment to decide which one to roll back to.
commands.beginnerNote: Without --record having been set on earlier apply commands, the CHANGE-CAUSE column is blank, making the history much less useful for figuring out what changed.

commands.title: Roll back a deployment
commands.useCase: The fastest way back to a known-good state after a bad deploy causes a production incident.
commands.beginnerNote: Only works if the ReplicaSet history for that revision still exists — Kubernetes keeps 10 revisions by default via revisionHistoryLimit.

commands.title: Pause a rollout
commands.useCase: Stop a rolling update mid-flight to make multiple manifest changes that should be applied together as one rollout.
commands.beginnerNote: A paused deployment will NOT respond to kubectl rollout undo — you must resume it first before any rollout command takes effect.

commands.title: Resume a paused rollout
commands.useCase: Continue a rollout after finishing a batch of changes made while it was paused.

commands.title: Restart all pods in a deployment
commands.useCase: Restart all pods to pick up a changed ConfigMap or Secret, since those changes do not automatically trigger a redeploy on their own.

commands.title: List services
commands.useCase: See what ClusterIP, NodePort, or LoadBalancer services exist and which ports they expose.
commands.beginnerNote: kubectl get svc is the common shorthand nearly every engineer uses day to day instead of typing the full word.

commands.title: List ingress objects
commands.useCase: Check which hostnames and paths are routed to which backend services.
commands.beginnerNote: An Ingress object alone does nothing without an Ingress controller installed in the cluster. This is the most common source of 'why is my Ingress not working' confusion.

commands.title: Expose a deployment as a service
commands.useCase: Quickly create a Service in front of a deployment so other pods in the cluster can reach it by a stable name.
commands.beginnerNote: Forgetting --target-port when the container listens on a different port than the Service causes silent traffic failures — connections just time out with no obvious error.

commands.title: Forward a local port to a pod
commands.useCase: Access a database or internal service running in the cluster from your local machine for debugging, without exposing it publicly.
commands.beginnerNote: The forward only lasts as long as the terminal session stays open, and it only proxies to that single pod — it does not load-balance across replicas.

commands.title: List network policies
commands.useCase: Audit which pods have traffic restrictions applied when investigating an unexpected connection failure or a security review.
commands.beginnerNote: A namespace with zero NetworkPolicies allows all traffic by default. Isolation only kicks in once at least one policy selects a pod — half-configured policies can be more dangerous than none.

commands.title: List ConfigMaps
commands.useCase: See what non-sensitive configuration objects exist in a namespace before checking what a specific pod is actually using.

commands.title: Create a ConfigMap

flags.description: Add a single key=value pair directly on the command line
Load one or more keys from the contents of a file, using the filename as the key
Bulk-load every KEY=VALUE line from an env-style file as separate keys

commands.useCase: Store application configuration, feature flags, or non-sensitive settings separately from the container image.
commands.beginnerNote: Imperatively created ConfigMaps are not in version control. Use --dry-run=client -o yaml to generate a manifest you can commit instead of creating it directly.

commands.title: List secrets
commands.useCase: See what Secret objects exist in a namespace, such as database credentials or TLS certificates.
commands.beginnerNote: Values are base64-encoded and hidden in the list output. Use kubectl get secret <name> -o jsonpath to decode a specific value instead of guessing from the list view.

commands.title: Create a generic Secret
commands.useCase: Store a database password, API key, or token for a pod to consume as an environment variable or mounted file.
commands.beginnerNote: Secrets are only base64-encoded, not encrypted, by default. Anyone with kubectl get/describe access on the Secret can decode the value instantly — treat RBAC on Secrets as your real protection.

commands.title: Create a TLS Secret
commands.useCase: Load a certificate and private key pair for an Ingress controller to terminate HTTPS traffic.

commands.title: Create a docker-registry Secret
commands.useCase: Authenticate the kubelet against a private container registry so it can pull images that require a login.
commands.beginnerNote: Creating the Secret is only half the fix — it does nothing until it's referenced by name in the pod spec's imagePullSecrets field.

commands.title: List PersistentVolumes
commands.useCase: Check which cluster-wide storage volumes exist and whether they're Bound, Available, or Released.
commands.beginnerNote: PersistentVolumes are cluster-scoped, not namespaced. Never pass -n to this command — it will simply be ignored since PVs don't belong to any namespace.

commands.title: List PersistentVolumeClaims
commands.useCase: Check whether a pod's storage request has been successfully bound to a volume before assuming a database pod's storage issue is elsewhere.
commands.beginnerNote: A PVC stuck in Pending almost always means no StorageClass can satisfy the requested size or access mode — check the storageClassName and requested capacity.

commands.title: Describe a PersistentVolumeClaim
commands.useCase: Read the Events section to find the exact provisioning failure reason when a PVC is stuck Pending.

commands.title: List storage classes
commands.useCase: Check which StorageClass is marked as default before creating a PVC that doesn't explicitly specify one.
commands.beginnerNote: Having zero or multiple default StorageClasses both cause confusing provisioning failures — with multiple defaults the behaviour depends on your cloud provider's admission controller.

commands.title: List roles and rolebindings
commands.useCase: Audit exactly what permissions exist within a single namespace during a security review or access debugging session.

commands.title: Create a Role
commands.useCase: Define a set of permissions, like read-only access to pods, scoped to a single namespace.
commands.beginnerNote: A Role only grants access within its own namespace no matter how it is bound — it can never grant cluster-wide access.

commands.title: Create a RoleBinding
commands.useCase: Actually grant the permissions defined in a Role to a specific user, group, or ServiceAccount.
commands.beginnerNote: A Role by itself grants nothing until it is bound to a subject via a RoleBinding — creating the Role alone is a no-op.

commands.title: Create a ClusterRole
commands.useCase: Define permissions that apply across every namespace, or to cluster-scoped resources like nodes and PersistentVolumes.

commands.title: Create a ClusterRoleBinding
commands.useCase: Grant a ServiceAccount or user permissions across the entire cluster, such as for a cluster-wide monitoring agent.
commands.beginnerNote: This is the broadest grant available in RBAC. A common security review finding is cluster-admin granted to a ServiceAccount that only ever needed read access to one namespace.

commands.title: Create a ServiceAccount
commands.useCase: Give a workload its own identity so RBAC permissions can be scoped precisely to what that workload needs, instead of relying on defaults.
commands.beginnerNote: Every namespace has a default ServiceAccount that pods use automatically if none is specified. Relying on it for anything with real permissions is a security smell.

commands.title: Check permissions

flags.description: Check what a different user or ServiceAccount is permitted to do, instead of yourself

commands.useCase: The fastest way to debug a 403 Forbidden error before hunting through Role and RoleBinding YAML by hand.

commands.title: Describe a resource
commands.useCase: The number one debugging command — shows the Events section that explains why something failed, not just its current status.
commands.beginnerNote: New users see CrashLoopBackOff in kubectl get pod and stop there. describe is where the actual reason lives — always run it next.

commands.title: View pod logs

flags.description: Stream logs continuously instead of printing a static snapshot
Show logs from the last terminated instance of the container, not the current one
Only show logs newer than a relative duration, e.g. 1h
Limit output to the last N lines
Specify which container's logs to show in a multi-container pod

commands.useCase: Read what an application actually printed before or during a failure — the single most-used debugging command after describe.
commands.beginnerNote: If the pod already restarted, plain kubectl logs shows the new container's empty logs. Use --previous to see why the last one crashed — this is the flag people forget at 2am.

commands.title: View cluster events

flags.description: Order events chronologically instead of the default, mostly-useless ordering

commands.useCase: See scheduling failures, probe failures, and image pull errors across a namespace in one chronological timeline.
commands.beginnerNote: Events expire after about an hour by default, so check this early during an incident — by the time you circle back the evidence may already be gone.

commands.title: Open a shell in a container
commands.useCase: Poke around inside a running container — check environment variables, test DNS, or inspect a mounted file directly.
commands.beginnerNote: Minimal and distroless images often have no shell at all. exec failing with 'executable file not found' usually means no shell exists, not that something is broken.

commands.title: Attach an ephemeral debug container

flags.description: Debug a disposable copy of the pod instead, if ephemeral containers aren't supported by the cluster

commands.useCase: Debug minimal or distroless images by attaching a separate debug container with tools like curl and busybox, without restarting the original workload.
commands.beginnerNote: Requires cluster support for ephemeral containers. If your cluster doesn't support it, use --copy-to to debug a disposable copy of the pod instead.

commands.title: View pod resource usage

flags.description: Break usage down per container instead of aggregating per pod

commands.useCase: Check live CPU and memory usage when investigating an OOMKilled pod or deciding whether to raise resource limits.
commands.beginnerNote: Silently fails with 'metrics not available' unless metrics-server is installed in the cluster — it is not part of Kubernetes core.

commands.title: View node resource usage
commands.useCase: Check overall cluster capacity pressure before assuming a scheduling failure is caused by something else.
commands.beginnerNote: Same metrics-server dependency as kubectl top pods — this fails the same way if metrics-server isn't installed.

commands.title: Wait for a condition
commands.useCase: In CI pipelines that need to block until a pod is Ready, instead of polling kubectl get in a loop.
commands.beginnerNote: Always set --timeout or this hangs indefinitely if the condition never becomes true, which can silently freeze a pipeline job.

commands.title: Delete all common resources in a namespace
commands.useCase: Clean up a temporary test namespace by removing pods, services, deployments and replicasets in one shot.
commands.beginnerNote: Same as kubectl get all — this only targets common resource types and will NOT delete ConfigMaps, Secrets, PVCs, or CRDs left behind.

commands.title: Extract fields with JSONPath
commands.useCase: Pull just the pod names, or any specific nested field, out of a large kubectl get output for use in a shell script.
commands.beginnerNote: Quoting JSONPath correctly differs between bash, zsh, and PowerShell. A mismatched quote is the most common reason these one-liners fail when copy-pasted between environments.

commands.title: Build a custom output table
commands.useCase: Build a readable table with exactly the fields you care about, for a status report or quick audit.

commands.title: Watch resources for changes
commands.useCase: Watch pods come up in real time during a rollout or scale-up, without repeatedly re-running kubectl get.
commands.beginnerNote: This command never exits on its own — you have to Ctrl-C out of it, which trips up scripts that call it expecting it to return.

commands.title: Preview an apply without changing anything

flags.description: Validate locally against the schema only — never contacts the cluster
Send the request to the API server for full validation, including admission webhooks, without persisting it

commands.useCase: Sanity check a manifest for syntax and schema errors before actually applying it to a shared cluster.
commands.beginnerNote: Client-side dry-run only validates locally and will not catch errors that require cluster state, like an admission webhook rejecting the resource.

commands.title: Diff a manifest against the live cluster
commands.useCase: See exactly what an apply would change before running it, useful in a CI gate that reviews infrastructure diffs.
commands.beginnerNote: Exits with a non-zero status code whenever there IS a difference, which can look like an error in scripts that don't account for it.

commands.title: Apply a Kustomize overlay
commands.useCase: Apply an environment-specific overlay of a base manifest, such as patching replica counts and image tags for production.
commands.beginnerNote: Kustomize support is built into kubectl since v1.14 with no separate install needed, which surprises people looking for a separate kustomize binary.

commands.title: Explain a resource field
commands.useCase: Look up what a specific manifest field does and what values it accepts, without leaving the terminal to check the docs.
